name: LuaJIT Bytecode
summary: LuaJIT's register-based bytecode with 32-bit instruction words (8-bit opcode, 8-bit destination register, 16-bit operand fields). Tuned for trace-JIT ergonomics and a hand-tuned interpreter; within 2× of native code on many benchmarks before the JIT fires.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
LuaJIT's bytecode is register-based with 32-bit instruction words: 8-bit opcode, 8-bit destination register, 16-bit operand fields. It's slightly different from standard Lua 5.x bytecode — LuaJIT tunes its instructions for trace-JIT ergonomics, with operations like `MOV` and arithmetic ops sized to fit interpreter dispatch into a tight loop.

The interpreter is written in hand-tuned assembly per architecture; the bytecode hits the cache predictably because the encoding is fixed-width.

sections.title: Interpreter throughput
sections.content:
LuaJIT's interpreter is one of the fastest interpreters ever built — within 2× of native code on many benchmarks before the JIT even fires. The bytecode design is a major reason: simple, predictable, dispatch-friendly. Fixed-width 32-bit instructions, dense operand encoding, and tight per-architecture assembly dispatch all compose into an interpreter whose per-instruction cost is dominated by the actual operation rather than dispatch overhead.

The lesson: a bytecode designed in coordination with its interpreter implementation is consistently faster than one designed against a generic dispatch model.
